fragments.content: Personality Disorders (PDs) are enduring patterns of inner experience and behavior deviating markedly from cultural expectations, pervasive across situations, inflexible, stable over time, leading to distress/impairment. Onset in adolescence/early adulthood. DSM-5 clusters: Cluster A (odd/eccentric)—Paranoid (distrust/suspiciousness), Schizoid (detachment, restricted affect), Schizotypal (acute discomfort with closeness, cognitive/perceptual distortions, eccentric behavior). Cluster B (dramatic/emotional/erratic)—Antisocial (disregard for others), Borderline (instability), Histrionic (attention-seeking), Narcissistic (grandiosity, need for admiration). Cluster C (anxious/fearful)—Avoidant (inhibition, inadequacy), Dependent (excessive need to be cared for), Obsessive-Compulsive PD (preoccupation with orderliness, perfectionism, control). Prevalence ~10-15% overall.

Psychotherapy is primary treatment. Borderline PD: DBT (strongest evidence), MBT, TFP, schema therapy. Antisocial PD: limited evidence, focus on substance use comorbidity, contingency management. Avoidant/Dependent/OCPD: CBT, schema therapy, psychodynamic approaches. Cluster A: supportive therapy, social skills training; antipsychotics may help cognitive-perceptual symptoms. No medications approved specifically for PDs; pharmacotherapy targets specific symptoms (mood stabilizers for impulsivity/affective instability, SSRIs for anxiety/depression, atypical antipsychotics for cognitive-perceptual symptoms). Avoid polypharmacy. Treatment engagement challenging; therapeutic alliance critical. Long-term perspective needed; personality change gradual. Comorbid conditions (depression, anxiety, SUD) require concurrent treatment.

DSM-5-TR general PD criteria: enduring pattern of inner experience/behavior deviating from cultural expectations in ≥2 areas (cognition, affectivity, interpersonal functioning, impulse control); inflexible/pervasive across situations; leads to clinically significant distress/impairment; stable over time (onset adolescence/early adulthood); not better explained by another mental disorder, substance effects, or medical condition. Specific criteria vary by PD type (e.g., Borderline requires ≥5 of 9 criteria including abandonment fears, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, suicidality, affective instability, emptiness, anger, paranoia/dissociation). DSM-5 includes Alternative Model for Personality Disorders (Section III) using dimensional approach (impairment in personality functioning + pathological traits).
